speaker: interviewer
text: Then on February 25th, 1997, by Nina Elazar-Wolff in Bat-Yam, Israel in the English language. So in the last cassette we were talking about coming back to so-called normal life in Riga in 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe what happens to you and the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Sad. Sadness. Lack of income. I can't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't dare to say to anyone that I can give lessons in foreign languages. Stalin's still alive. Grishe has lost his job in the hospital because of me, works as a doctor in a factory, gets 70 rubles per month which is not enough to make your ends meet. Plus our nanny's pension. So we somehow we make ends meet, but we are very often hungry. All of us live in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the nanny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And no friends, no relatives. Grishe's relatives have also deserted us. They're rich, don't want to hear anything from a person who has had a-- who has had a prison sentence or has been to prison for political reasons. This was a Jewish attitude by the way. Can't say anything nice about these people. And later, little by little, little by little, we start living. I get some pupils, become pregnant. Naomi was born. And after Naomi's birth there's a history of the Jewish doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: '52. And we are already getting ready for Siberia again. That was also a very hard time for my family, and Naomi was lying in her diapers. I was called by a Russian woman whom I didn't know. And she told-- she told me she was coming to pick me up. Somebody wants to talk to me. And I understood immediately it's from the KGB. And I left my baby with my neighbor. I phoned Grishe at work and I said [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He understood immediately. And I was taken to a private house, where I was kept for hours by a Russian man, an investigator. So he was a man who offered me-- if I don't want to go to prison again, he offered me very good salary to provide my family in Riga-- was a good salary-- to go to Sweden and to work for the KGB.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did a very clever thing that moment. It lasted four or five hours, the conversation. I was terribly upset and terribly nervous, and he saw it. And the woman who had taken me to that place is a doctor from the KGB. She wanted to see my reactions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, if you want me to get back to the place where I had been living with my baby, let's do the lunatic asylum. You can send me by force, but I won't to do anything for you. But that was also a terrible adventure for me. So and such adventures repeatedly occurred until 1960, when I did an investigation, which lasted four months. That was-- I believe that was the end of my common sense and of my ability to think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you go into Jewish life or normal life--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was in Jewish life all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: After '60, did you start living more or less as everybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was always frightened. I was always afraid. I was afraid of people. There were only certain people who had access to my house because I didn't trust them. And least of all, I trusted Soviet Jews who were living in Riga, because many of them were KGB agents. Well, I had certain people. I had some friends who were opera singers and musicians, artists, and who I knew exactly that they are my friends. That's all I could rely on them, and I was-- I was right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing during all those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which years?

speaker: interviewer
text: From '60 till your aliyah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. So doing-- eh, for a, for a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Living?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a living, I gave lessons. Then I gave lessons. I was not afraid to give lessons, because the time had changed, you see? The atmosphere had changed. I was giving the French these English and German lessons without being frightened. I was offered to tut-- teach at school at a institute. And I said that I'm not going to teach at this--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children grew up. My children grew up in a very Zionist Jewish atmosphere on my side. As you know, I have given my children Jewish names, Shulamit and Naomi, which nobody did in Riga those days. I wanted them to be-- to remember their Jewish people. I remember when Naomi was in the cradle, I sang to her. I knew they will take me away again. [HEBREW] I was sitting and crying bitterly. And I thought, never ever will my child know she's Jewish, because she will have no mother. And now you see she has a mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when did you arrive in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, in April. Was the 26th of April, 1971, the happiest day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember your feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beyond description. Absolutely. Only when I saw Coca-Cola in Vienna. I believed that I am-- that I am not in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to go through a big struggle to go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I knew I would never be let out. And I was told I would never be let out. We got offered money. We had to pay. And I was happy there was somebody to whom I could pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Vaboulis after--

speaker: interviewee
text: They--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: He and his wife [INAUDIBLE] took us to Moscow, and until we-- and we said goodbye to each other. And we are in touch, what means-- He lives at-- he's an old man now. He's 83 and very ill. But he has a nice-- a nice home, and [INAUDIBLE] is a very good wife to him. And we-- we correspond. He knows that I will never, ever come to Riga. He'd never suggest I come to Riga, and helping him financially as much as I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he finish the 10 years that he got-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did. Yes, he came home very sick man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see him immediately when he came-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: He came directly to my house from the railway station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact during all those years when he was in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now and again, a postcard because he could write only once in six months. He was not in jail. He was in a hard labor camp, which is worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know about your marriage to Grishe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. He knew everything, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually, when did you marry Grishe, and when did you divorce Vaboulis?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't divorce Vaboulis. That was automatic as the KGB divorced people, political prisoners, as soon as they were arrested. I was just-- a paper was read to me that on a certain date I was separated from him, and that's it. Grishe I met-- I don't know when I met him. I really don't know. When Shulamit went to school, so because she needed a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now I understand that in Israel you made a sort of a new life. Is it true?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Officially, what were you doing, and Grishe what was doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Grishe has been working as a doctor in many places-- in many places same time I mean. First in [NON-ENGLISH] when we arrived and then in [NON-ENGLISH]. For 25 years he has been working. And I was giving private lessons. So I could help my children graduate from university. I could help my grandchildren and still can help my grandchildren. And I'm very happy about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I would like to come back in your stories and to dedicate a chapter to all this Latvian fascist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember you said that you swear you made a [NON-ENGLISH] that you are going to hunt them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would like to dedicate the next chapter to-- to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when did you decide to hunt-- if you come alive out of this hell of the Shoah, do you remember taking this decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it exactly. It was the night. There was a heavy snowfall on the 8th of December, when I felt very restless hiding in Vaboulis' room. He was not at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, it so happened that they found out that my family was killed, yeah? And when I-- this was confirmed, I gave an oath to the dead that as long-- so long as I live, I will try to find them, to take them to court, to have them sentenced if possible. And this is what I did. I kept word. So the first time you could do something was about this subject was in Sweden when you have your report?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not exactly. I started with it in Riga when I was pregnant with Shulamit in 1948. Shortly before I gave birth to her, I met in the street a Latvian man who was my neighbor in Goldingen and who led the killing of all Goldingen Jews, [INAUDIBLE]. I thought he was in Germany, but he was in Riga. He jumped onto the train car. I jumped after him, and I accompanied him. He didn't notice me. He got off. I got off. He went into the house. I went after him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to [INAUDIBLE], and she took me for a Latvian. I said, you know, I know somewhere in this house is my friend [INAUDIBLE] must be living. Perhaps you know where he lives? And she told me where he lives, in her house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I have a friend who was in the [INAUDIBLE]. It was a special organization who were looking for war criminals and for war crime-- punishing war crime, and I told him the story. He's from Goldingen. The man was not arrested. He had-- he had been in prison in [INAUDIBLE] but he permits them to work as a KGB agent, and he was released. And this was a very bad feeling, my first bad feeling about the war crime and the Russians. They didn't care if Jews were killed or if they were not. So started in-- in the full sense meaning of the word in-- in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where actually and when did you start being really involved and active in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Israel, of course. That was 1976 when Arajs was arrested, when I went to the police department here for Nazi crime, department for Nazi crime and I told them my story. And I remained in touch with them about the other-- about the rest of the-- of the gangsters. And I gave them all the information I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Israeli police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the first place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happens? You go and give them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Evidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Evidence, testimonies--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was farther to the [INAUDIBLE] countries. They-- they informed Germany about Arajs. I am summoned to-- to go to-- on the 5th of July to go to Hamburg--

speaker: interviewer
text: 5th of--

speaker: interviewee
text: 5th of May to Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '78. And I go there, Naomi accompanies me. We're spending there 10 days. 10 days, I'm testifying there against him. I'm sitting in the same courtroom he is sitting. He's acting as a sick man, and he's not. He's taken very good care of. He keeps quiet all the time. He doesn't speak to me. There is no eye contact between us. And I'm asked-- I'm not asked questions. They didn't-- The jury didn't ask me questions in the beginning. They had me tell my story, same story I'm telling you now, more or less, I'm telling them. And then only, they start asking me questions. And so everything seems to be correct. A moment comes when the presiding judge, Dr. Wagner, says-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. Here they are. This is hard to believe every word you are saying, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but then he was a-- a young man. Now before you an-- an old, ailing man is sitting. Could you identify him? Who asked you? Come again. Who asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The presiding judge, Dr. Wagner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. W-A-G-N-E-R. [SIGHS] So I'm saying my [NON-ENGLISH], my sister's photo. I put on my glasses. Go ahead. Pointing at him. That's him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which picture were you shown at the court? Which kind of picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a-- a small photo. And so the photo they showed me was very big. It's about 30 people were sitting, uniformed people were [INAUDIBLE] all wearing the same uniform, and he was among them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was silence. No reaction. Did he react in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was not looking at him. He was not sitting across from me. He was sitting the same line, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the courtroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: A usual courtrooms as we see on television. I would say the buildings are like in Riga. And the-- the interiors are also a very usual courtroom. Security was-- was strict on-- on entering. There were some German girls standing, and they were checking our handbags and from top to toe. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you housed while being in Hamburg? Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a Norwegian-- at [NON-ENGLISH] A Norwegian hotel, yes, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel-- how did you feel there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany? I felt nothing absolutely, because I had no-- no contact with the-- with the outer world. In the hotel, people were very friendly. They knew we had come for a trial. And the jury was very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I've got nothing to add. No contact with people. Did you feel any anti feeling or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --any pro feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No. Pro, yes. I don't even know whether I should mention it. There were two-- two trials here in Israel later, because the [NON-ENGLISH] were not pleased with the outcome of the trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of the judges said on-- when the trial was over waving his hand to all courtroom, Shalom, Mrs. Hait, saying goodbye, farewell, because I heard that he was leaving. He didn't want to take part in such trials anymore. He couldn't bear it. Who was the prosecutor in Arajs' trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lother Klemm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. L-O-T-H-E-R, Klemm, K-L-E-M-M. A youngish man. Very friendly, very candid, trying to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a good contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sincere contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who defended Mr. Arajs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Fritz Steinaecker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. F-R-I-T-Z. Steinaecker, S-T-E-I-N-A-E-C-K-E-R. Steinaecker. From Frankfurt he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe please a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very capable man.  Very capable man. He was correct. He was polite as all Germans are. I didn't-- I didn't have the feeling that he was trying to do me any harm in that. He just wanted to earn his money, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he came-- when he-- On one occasion, he thought, he felt that he had lost the case. When Arajs-- he said-- at the end-- the end of the trial, but how did you know that what was done to you was done by Arajs? So I said, he said to me, you bitch. Do you know who's standing before you? I'm Viktors Arajs. He said, [GERMAN], I have no more questions. And that was the end. But here in Israel during the interval, he came up to me and said, Mrs. Hait, why are you so hostile to me? I said, yes I am. But why? I said, it's either ideologically or financially interested that you tried to set a war criminal free. And in both cases, it's appalling. He didn't say a word. That was the last time you spoke to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other judges on the-- on the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Put them here on the table, the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, if you don't remember now, it's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE] or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the security point of view, did you feel secure there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. I didn't feel-- in America, security was very strict and very reliable. In Germany, I did not feel any security at all. Perhaps there was one, but we were not aware of it. We were also-- we were not told that there is security. For instance, once-- on one occasion, we were downstairs, we saw an old man sit there as if drunk. So I said-- no, Naomi said to me, so this may be a Latvian, you know, just watching us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the trial-- you mentioned lasted for 10 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many sessions a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly-- no, there was one session a day only. One session a day, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in which language did you give your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. I prefer German, because I didn't want any translator and it was not needed. And I think they were also satisfied because makes things easier.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- Can-- can you recall how did you feel every day or at the end of the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I knew I have to be strong, I knew I have to do my job. I knew that it has to be proven that this man is the greatest murderer of eastern Europe during the war, that's he responsible for the killing of Latvian Jewry and he has to spend his life in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you took Naomi, your younger daughter with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she relate to the whole thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first place, I didn't take her with me. She suggested to come with me in order to protect me and to help me. Like me, I believe, she helped me a lot. I wouldn't have been able to do the job without that moral help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe one day in the court or one big impression that you had there or incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my biggest impressions is the moment I went up to identify the man. Second one to answer the question by Steinaecker, why I thought it was Arajs. And there were actually no other special incidents that are to be remembered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any audience in the court?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was the worst part of the trial. They had not published-- that the German authorities that a war-- an infamous war criminal is going to be punished. No publicity at all. We were all alone there. A Latvian woman over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Latvian woman. We don't know, but she was a person who was interested to find out the truth. And then there was a Jewish family and a husband, wife, and son. I don't know their name anymore. Their son was Benny, who had served in Israel in [INAUDIBLE] unit. But since the lady herself and her husband are victims of the Holocaust and she had been sterilized, they were afraid to lose their son, so he came back. They were forthcoming and friendly before the trial also. They were standing in the corridor waiting for us. They were there. And there was a correspondent who later wrote an article.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the local paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 days after we had arrived, so it must have been on the 14th I believe or the 15th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any holidays, Jewish holidays while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yom Ha'atzmaut, Independence Day. Can't speak about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: It hurts.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. You don't have to. Did you have any time off there while you were in the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a lot of time off, but we spent it in the hotel room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were not busy in the court?

speaker: interviewee
text: --sleeping, talking, reading, nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have a lot of preparations for the court-- for the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the trial? Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of homework to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trying to recall facts of the past, trying to recall not only Arajs and his deeds, but people associated with all these terrible acts and dates and different moments. I did it at night. I did it always alone and at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other people from the Jewish community, except this couple, try to get in touch with you to establish contact with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There are no local Hamburg Jews anymore. There are Jews from Iran selling carpets, so what have they got to do with the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you look backwards when you try to place yourself at that time, how did the whole trial affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Satisfaction. Moral satisfaction. My duty carried out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you come back to Israel mid-May.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was the-- What was the sentence of this trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know anything about it. Only 1979, I get a phone call from our local police telling me that [NON-ENGLISH] are displeased with the trial. They want a second trial, and they suggest I come to Germany on a certain date. That was by phone. I refused immediately and said categorically I have got nothing new to tell them. Whatever I had, I have told them. Tell the gentlemen I have an address in Bat-Yam so and so, and they can come and visit me. And they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a trial in-- in Tel Aviv, in [NON-ENGLISH] near the museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you assist?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go-- did you go to listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- they came to me because of me, not-- there was not a trial. It was an investigation and a cross-examination. But Steinaecker was here, the lawyer, and et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to Arajs at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in prison after 10 years of imprisonment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kind of a sentence did he get?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got-- since there is no capital punishment, he got a life sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he died 10 years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. What-- what were your feelings towards the Germans, generally speaking, after the whole thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My feelings towards the Germans have not changed. Absolutely. A trial can't change my feelings towards them. I find-- I find them guilty for crime against humanity, particularly against my people. And when I saw now on television the [NON-ENGLISH] in Frankfurt, it only proves that I am right despite all the-- all the sorts of my closest friends who say that there's a different Germany now.

speaker: interviewer
text: We come to the end of this cassette, and we'll take on the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shalom.
